news_summary: Today's environmental news highlights innovative recycling efforts by retailers, the rise of electric energy shaping the future, urban areas supporting wildflower growth, and new scientific insights into Earth's deep geological dynamics.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les initiatives innovantes des commerçants en matière de recyclage, l'essor de l'énergie électrique qui façonne l'avenir, les zones urbaines qui favorisent la croissance des fleurs sauvages, ainsi que de nouvelles découvertes scientifiques sur la dynamique géologique profonde de la Terre.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan las innovadoras iniciativas de reciclaje de los comercios minoristas, el auge de la energía eléctrica que está marcando el futuro, las zonas urbanas que favorecen el crecimiento de las flores silvestres y los nuevos descubrimientos científicos sobre la dinámica geológica profunda de la Tierra.

articles.title: 7 Retailers With Impressive Recycling Programs
articles.summary:
Forty thousand miles of plastic waste wash through the global ocean every year, enough to...
The post 7 Retailers With Impressive Recycling Programs appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Forty thousand miles of plastic waste wash through the global ocean every year, enough to wrap the Earth at the equator. But walk into the right store, and you can personally shorten that pipeline by a few feet, returning a pair of worn sneakers, a dead laptop, or a piece of furniture destined for the dumpster.

Some retailers have built genuine end-of-life infrastructure for the products they sell — not just a PR line, but real systems with documented results. The seven below have the numbers to back it up, updated for 2026.

Patagonia

Patagonia’s Worn Wear program remains one of the most comprehensive take-back systems in retail apparel. In 2025, customers made more than 137,000 trade-ins — almost 71,000 of them from return and warranty claims — and the online Shop Used feature launched in September 2024 has expanded the secondhand market significantly. Items deemed wearable are cleaned, repaired, and resold through Worn Wear; those beyond repair enter a recycling pipeline.

On the material innovation side, Patagonia partnered with Eastman in 2024 to process 8,000 pounds of pre- and post-consumer clothing waste through molecular recycling — breaking apparel down to chemical building blocks for reuse as new fiber. The brand has also moved aggressively on materials: by fall 2025, over 90 percent of Patagonia’s fabrics were recycled, organic, or traceable. Its 2025 Work in Progress Report disclosed that reducing hang tags by over 40 million pieces has avoided 170,000 pounds of packaging waste. The structural challenge — mechanically recycling blended fabrics — remains unsolved at industrial scale, and Patagonia acknowledges it openly.

Apple

Apple’s trade-in and recycling program sent 15.9 million devices to new owners through refurbishment schemes in 2024 alone. Devices that cannot be refurbished are processed by Daisy, Apple’s disassembly robot, which can now break down 36 models of iPhone into discrete components to recover aluminum, copper, rare earth elements, and other materials. A second robot, Dave, disassembles Taptic Engines to recover rare earth magnets, tungsten, and steel.

The material-recovery numbers are striking. In 2024, 24 percent of all materials shipped in Apple products came from recycled or renewable sources, up from 10 percent in 2019. Recycled aluminum accounted for 71 percent of the aluminum Apple purchased. The company avoided 6.2 million metric tons of greenhouse gas emissions by using recycled and low-carbon materials in 2024, according to its 2025 Environmental Progress Report. Apple has also surpassed 99 percent on its 2025 goal to use 100 percent recycled rare earth elements in all magnets and 100 percent recycled cobalt in all Apple-designed batteries. Customers can drop devices off at any Apple Store or ship for free.

Best Buy

Best Buy has collected 2.7 billion pounds of electronics and appliances since launching its recycling program in 2009, making it the nation’s largest retail collector of e-waste. The program accepts most consumer electronics at more than 1,000 stores regardless of where items were purchased, collecting more than 400 pounds of product every minute stores are open.

The program has expanded: a mail-in recycling service now lets customers without easy store access ship old tech in purpose-built boxes. A home haul-away service launched for customers who cannot transport large items. Best Buy requires all recycling partners to comply with rigorous environmental management standards and holds them to regulatory compliance and responsible workforce practices. TVs and monitors carry a $25 fee; most other electronics — phones, laptops, tablets, cables — are accepted free.

Nike

Nike’s original Reuse-a-Shoe program launched in 1995 to recycle worn athletic footwear into Nike Grind material for surfaces and new products has evolved into the Recycling + Donation (RAD) service, now available globally.

The program accepts athletic footwear and apparel from any brand and inspects each item to determine donation or recycling eligibility. Wearable items go to nonprofit partners including Soles4Souls for redistribution to communities in need; worn-out footwear is ground down into Nike Grind, which goes into playground surfaces, running tracks, and new Nike products.

Part of Nike’s Move to Zero initiative, targeting zero carbon and zero waste across the supply chain, the Participating stores accept shoes of any brand — athletic footwear only; no cleats, boots, or sandals. Nike also runs Nike Refurbished, which cleans and resells gently worn or slightly imperfect footwear and apparel at select factory and community stores, extending product life before material recovery.

Staples

Staples pioneered national retail recycling in 2007 as the first U.S. retailer to offer a universal e-waste takeback program. Today the program accepts over 50 types of materials including computers, printers, phones, cables, batteries, crayons, and coffee machines from any brand. Since 2021, Staples has recycled 7,000 tons of e-waste and 19 million ink and toner cartridges, helping HP reach a milestone of 1 billion cartridges recycled.

Staples’ Easy Rewards program currently gives members 500 points (equivalent to $5 back) per month for tech recycling. Ink and toner cartridge recycling earns $2 per cartridge for members spending at least $30 on ink over the previous 180 days, up to a monthly limit. Staples uses certified recyclers whenever possible, and recycled toner material gets routed into road construction aggregate. The company accepts electronics in-store at customer service desks at all U.S. Staples locations.

IKEA

Furniture is the United States’ largest category of discarded household goods, with Americans throwing away approximately 12 million tons of it each year. IKEA’s Buyback & Resell program addresses the problem at the point of sale: customers fill out an online form, receive a value estimate, and bring gently used IKEA furniture to any participating store in exchange for store credit. Items that pass inspection enter the As-Is section for resale; those that cannot be resold are recycled under IKEA’s zero-waste-to-landfill policy.

The U.S. program now runs in 33 stores and, as of 2025, accepts more than 5,000 product types, including tables, chairs, storage units, lamps, and kids’ furniture among many. Globally, IKEA’s circular initiatives contributed to a 24.3 percent reduction in the company’s climate footprint while revenue grew 30.9 percent. Sofas, mattresses, and modified products are not accepted. IKEA Family members currently receive 50 percent more in store credit through May 2026.

REI

REI’s Re/Supply program sold nearly 1.4 million items of used outdoor gear in 2024, double the volume from 2019. The program accepts trade-ins of gently used REI-brand and name-brand gear including backpacks, sleeping bags, tents, and apparel. Members receive store credit; items are inspected, cleaned, and resold at a discount. Selling a used item through Re/Supply emits at least 50 percent less carbon than selling a new equivalent, even accounting for shipping, cleaning, and remerchandising.

REI also became the first major U.S. retailer to reach 90 percent operational waste diversion, achieving zero-waste certification in 2024 that audited and independently verified — ahead of Walmart and Target. Three of its distribution centers hold TRUE Zero Waste certification. In 2024, about 52 percent of the polyester and 45 percent of the nylon in REI Co-op products came from recycled sources. REI also charges brand partners a recycling fee to discourage individual plastic poly bags, and the majority of brands it carries have eliminated them as standard practice.

Related Reading

Editor’s Note: Originally written by Sarah Lozanova on April 10, 2017, this article was substantially updated in April 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant, large-scale recycling programs by major retailers that have measurable positive impacts on reducing waste and promoting sustainability. These programs collectively contribute to reducing plastic and electronic waste, advancing circular economy practices, and lowering carbon footprints, benefiting society and the environment broadly. The article provides detailed context and data on each retailer's efforts, demonstrating real-world environmental progress.
articles.personality_title: Seven major retailers making big strides in recycling and waste reduction
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, about 40,000 miles of plastic waste flow into the world’s oceans, enough to circle the Earth at the equator. This waste comes from many sources, including old clothes, electronics, and furniture that are thrown away.

**What happened** – Seven well-known retailers have created strong recycling programs to handle the products they sell. For example, Patagonia takes back worn clothes and recycles fabric; Apple collects millions of old devices and uses robots to recycle valuable materials; Best Buy recycles billions of pounds of electronics; Nike recycles old shoes into new products; Staples accepts many types of electronics and cartridges; IKEA buys back and resells used furniture; and REI sells used outdoor gear and has cut waste in its operations.

**Impact** – These programs help reduce trash going to landfills and oceans by giving old products a new life. For instance, Apple recycled enough materials in 2024 to avoid 6.2 million tons of greenhouse gases. Patagonia’s program keeps tens of thousands of clothes from being wasted. IKEA’s furniture buyback program helps keep millions of tons of furniture out of the trash. These efforts also encourage people to recycle by making it easy to return old items.

**What's next step** – Retailers will likely expand these programs, improve recycling technology, and find ways to recycle more types of materials. For example, Patagonia is working on recycling mixed fabrics, and Apple continues to improve its robot recycling. More stores may offer mail-in or pickup services to make recycling even easier for customers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Major retailers are developing effective recycling programs that turn old products into new resources, reducing waste and helping the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Sept grands détaillants qui avancent fortement dans le recyclage et la réduction des déchets
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, environ 40 000 miles de déchets plastiques se déversent dans les océans du monde, assez pour faire le tour de la Terre à l'équateur. Ces déchets proviennent de nombreuses sources, notamment des vêtements, des appareils électroniques et des meubles jetés.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Sept détaillants bien connus ont mis en place de solides programmes de recyclage pour gérer les produits qu'ils vendent. Par exemple, Patagonia récupère les vêtements usagés et recycle les tissus ; Apple collecte des millions d'appareils anciens et utilise des robots pour recycler les matériaux précieux ; Best Buy recycle des milliards de livres d'électronique ; Nike recycle les vieilles chaussures en nouveaux produits ; Staples accepte de nombreux types d'électronique et de cartouches ; IKEA rachète et revend des meubles d'occasion ; et REI vend du matériel de plein air usagé et a réduit ses déchets.

**Impact** – Ces programmes aident à réduire les déchets envoyés en décharge ou dans les océans en donnant une nouvelle vie aux anciens produits. Par exemple, Apple a recyclé suffisamment de matériaux en 2024 pour éviter 6,2 millions de tonnes de gaz à effet de serre. Le programme de Patagonia empêche des dizaines de milliers de vêtements d'être gaspillés. Le programme de rachat de meubles d'IKEA aide à garder des millions de tonnes de meubles hors des déchets. Ces efforts encouragent aussi les gens à recycler en facilitant le retour des anciens objets.

**Prochaine étape** – Les détaillants vont probablement étendre ces programmes, améliorer la technologie de recyclage et trouver des moyens de recycler plus de types de matériaux. Par exemple, Patagonia travaille sur le recyclage des tissus mélangés, et Apple continue d'améliorer ses robots de recyclage. Plus de magasins pourraient offrir des services d'envoi postal ou de ramassage pour faciliter encore plus le recyclage.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les grands détaillants développent des programmes de recyclage efficaces qui transforment les anciens produits en nouvelles ressources, réduisant les déchets et aidant l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Siete grandes minoristas que avanzan mucho en reciclaje y reducción de residuos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, alrededor de 40,000 millas de desechos plásticos llegan a los océanos del mundo, suficiente para rodear la Tierra en el ecuador. Estos desechos provienen de muchas fuentes, incluyendo ropa vieja, aparatos electrónicos y muebles desechados.

**Qué pasó** – Siete minoristas conocidos han creado fuertes programas de reciclaje para manejar los productos que venden. Por ejemplo, Patagonia recoge ropa usada y recicla telas; Apple recolecta millones de dispositivos viejos y usa robots para reciclar materiales valiosos; Best Buy recicla miles de millones de libras de electrónica; Nike recicla zapatos viejos en nuevos productos; Staples acepta muchos tipos de electrónicos y cartuchos; IKEA compra y revende muebles usados; y REI vende equipo de exterior usado y ha reducido sus desechos.

**Impacto** – Estos programas ayudan a reducir la basura que va a los vertederos y océanos al darle nueva vida a los productos viejos. Por ejemplo, Apple recicló suficientes materiales en 2024 para evitar 6.2 millones de toneladas de gases de efecto invernadero. El programa de Patagonia evita que decenas de miles de prendas se desperdicien. El programa de recompra de muebles de IKEA ayuda a mantener millones de toneladas de muebles fuera de la basura. Estos esfuerzos también animan a las personas a reciclar al facilitar la devolución de artículos viejos.

**Próximo paso** – Es probable que los minoristas amplíen estos programas, mejoren la tecnología de reciclaje y encuentren formas de reciclar más tipos de materiales. Por ejemplo, Patagonia trabaja en reciclar telas mezcladas, y Apple sigue mejorando sus robots de reciclaje. Más tiendas podrían ofrecer servicios de envío postal o recogida para facilitar aún más el reciclaje.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Los grandes minoristas están desarrollando programas de reciclaje efectivos que convierten productos viejos en nuevos recursos, reduciendo residuos y ayudando al medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant, interconnected marketplace scene featuring symbolic representations of seven distinct recycling stations shaped like a sneaker, a smartphone, a laptop, a furniture chair, a printer, an athletic shoe, and outdoor gear, all gently glowing and linked by flowing ribbons of recycled materials weaving through natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: The ‘age of electricity’ is here. No one knows what comes next.
articles.content:
The war launched by the United States and Israel on Iran has caused an unprecedented disruption in global energy markets, bottlenecking 20 percent of the world’s supply of oil and liquefied natural gas. We don’t yet know exactly what this means for the fight against climate change. But, thanks to two new reports released this week, we now have the clearest picture yet of the path the world was on before the conflict sent the price of oil soaring — and it was a path where the fossil fuels threatened by the war were less central than ever to meeting growing global energy needs.

The world is entering an “age of electricity,” according to the reports, which come from the International Energy Agency, or IEA, an intergovernmental organization that publishes the world’s most authoritative analyses on the global energy sector, and the think tank Ember. That’s because core economic activities that traditionally involve burning oil and gas — driving cars, heating buildings, and even running industrial processes like steelmaking — are increasingly powered by electricity instead. And, most importantly for the climate fight, an ever-larger share of that electricity is coming from renewable sources.

The two new analyses confirmed that 2025 was a banner year for renewable energy. Solar power was the single biggest source used to meet humanity’s growing appetite for electricity. New power generation from the broader suite of carbon-free sources — including wind, nuclear, and hydropower — actually exceeded the overall rise in electricity demand, meaning renewables began to displace fossil fuel sources. If this trend sticks, it would mean that the so-called energy transition meant to shepherd humanity out of the climate crisis is no longer theoretical.

“This was a year when the economy boomed, electricity demand grew very healthily — and still all that demand growth was met with renewables,” said Daan Walter, a lead researcher at Ember.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

In 2025, renewables edged out coal in global electricity generation for the first time in more than a century. This progress was fueled by China and India, the world’s two most populous countries that together comprise 42 percent of global fossil power generation. The nations both saw electricity generated by fossil fuels fall in the same year for the first time this century. Like other countries around the world, China and India have been rapidly building out solar, wind, and battery infrastructure. (The cost of batteries fell 45 percent in 2025, an even steeper decline than the 20 percent drop in costs that analysts tracked in 2024.)

There’s another sign that 2025 marked a turning point in the energy transition, according to the Ember report: Unlike in past years, the plateau in fossil fuel use was not tied to a recession. Global economic growth last year was normal, which indicates that renewable energy is driving a structural trend away from fossil fuels when it comes to generating electricity.

But that doesn’t mean that oil, gas, and coal use are nearing extinction. When it comes to the broader energy economy, rather than just electricity generation, the IEA’s report finds that renewables still aren’t displacing fossil fuels fast enough to force a sustained decline in the world’s use of greenhouse-gas-emitting energy. (This is because not all energy — for instance that which currently powers jets, cargo ships, and many motor vehicles — is generated from electricity.)

As a result of complications like these, global carbon dioxide emissions reached a record high last year, rising 0.4 percent from 2024 levels. The pace of the increase, however, is declining as renewables rise. For years, emissions declines were driven by developed countries like the United States and European Union member states. Last year, however, emissions from advanced economies grew faster than emissions from developing countries for the first time since the 1990s, according to the IEA.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The trend reversal was driven by the U.S., where coal demand rose 10 percent last year. Rising natural gas prices prompted power producers to switch back to coal, which had been displaced by fracked natural gas in recent years. Plus, electricity use rose thanks to a harsh winter across much of the eastern part of the country, as well as the rollout of industrial-scale power customers like the data centers needed for new artificial intelligence applications.

But trends in the opposite direction in developing countries played a role, too. In Indonesia, for example, electric cars now comprise more than 15 percent of new car sales — a larger share than in the United States and up from virtually 0 percent in the early 2020s. Many customers are “leapfrogging” gasoline-powered cars altogether and purchasing an EV as their first vehicle.

“The energy transition was conceived as something that is led by the developed world, and the developing world kind of hobbles after at a slower pace,” said Walter. “We’re now seeing ‘leapfrogging’ across the world where actually developing economies are going faster in many ways than developed economies.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant progress in the global energy transition toward renewable electricity, highlighting 2025 as a pivotal year where renewables surpassed coal in electricity generation, driven by large-scale adoption in populous countries like China and India. It emphasizes a structural shift away from fossil fuels in electricity production despite geopolitical disruptions, with measurable benefits for climate action and a broad global impact. The detailed data and analysis provide substantial context on this positive environmental trend.
articles.personality_title: Renewable energy surpasses coal in 2025, marking a new age of electricity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The world’s energy systems are shifting as countries try to reduce pollution and fight climate change. Traditionally, much of the energy people use came from burning fossil fuels like coal, oil, and gas. But these fuels release harmful gases that warm the planet.

**What happened** – In 2025, renewable energy sources like solar, wind, and hydropower generated more electricity worldwide than coal for the first time in over 100 years. This change was led by big countries such as China and India, which together make up a large part of global energy use. Both saw a drop in electricity made from fossil fuels while increasing clean energy. Also, the cost of batteries, important for storing renewable energy, fell sharply.

**Impact** – This shift shows that the world is moving toward an “age of electricity” where more activities are powered by clean electricity instead of burning fossil fuels. It means renewables are not just a small part of the energy mix anymore—they are taking the lead. This helps lower pollution and slows climate change. However, fossil fuels are still used in many areas like airplanes and ships, so the transition isn’t complete.

**What's next step** – Experts will watch if this trend continues and spreads to other parts of the energy system beyond electricity. Countries will also work to replace fossil fuels in transportation and industry. Meanwhile, developing countries are adopting electric cars faster than some rich countries, showing new ways to speed up the clean energy shift.

**One-sentence takeaway** – In 2025, renewable energy overtook coal for electricity worldwide, marking a clear move toward cleaner power and a changing global energy future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les énergies renouvelables dépassent le charbon en 2025, marquant une nouvelle ère de l’électricité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les systèmes énergétiques mondiaux évoluent alors que les pays cherchent à réduire la pollution et à lutter contre le changement climatique. Traditionnellement, une grande partie de l’énergie utilisée provenait de la combustion de combustibles fossiles comme le charbon, le pétrole et le gaz, qui libèrent des gaz nocifs réchauffant la planète.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, les sources d’énergie renouvelable comme le solaire, l’éolien et l’hydroélectricité ont produit plus d’électricité que le charbon dans le monde pour la première fois depuis plus de 100 ans. Ce changement a été mené par de grands pays comme la Chine et l’Inde, qui représentent une grande partie de la consommation mondiale d’énergie. Ces deux pays ont vu une baisse de leur électricité produite à partir de combustibles fossiles tout en augmentant leur énergie propre. Le coût des batteries, essentielles pour stocker l’énergie renouvelable, a aussi fortement diminué.

**Impact** – Ce changement montre que le monde entre dans une « ère de l’électricité » où de plus en plus d’activités sont alimentées par une électricité propre plutôt que par la combustion de combustibles fossiles. Cela signifie que les énergies renouvelables ne sont plus une petite partie du mix énergétique, elles prennent la tête. Cela aide à réduire la pollution et à ralentir le changement climatique. Cependant, les combustibles fossiles sont encore utilisés dans des secteurs comme l’aviation et le transport maritime, donc la transition n’est pas terminée.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts vont surveiller si cette tendance se poursuit et s’étend à d’autres secteurs énergétiques au-delà de l’électricité. Les pays travailleront aussi à remplacer les combustibles fossiles dans les transports et l’industrie. Par ailleurs, les pays en développement adoptent les voitures électriques plus vite que certains pays riches, montrant de nouvelles façons d’accélérer la transition énergétique.

**Phrase clé** – En 2025, les énergies renouvelables ont dépassé le charbon pour la production d’électricité dans le monde, marquant un tournant clair vers une énergie plus propre et un avenir énergétique mondial en mutation.
articles.personality_title_es: Las energías renovables superan al carbón en 2025, marcando una nueva era eléctrica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los sistemas energéticos del mundo están cambiando mientras los países intentan reducir la contaminación y combatir el cambio climático. Tradicionalmente, gran parte de la energía que usamos proviene de quemar combustibles fósiles como el carbón, el petróleo y el gas, que liberan gases dañinos que calientan el planeta.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, las fuentes de energía renovable como la solar, eólica e hidroeléctrica generaron más electricidad en todo el mundo que el carbón por primera vez en más de 100 años. Este cambio fue liderado por países grandes como China e India, que juntos representan una gran parte del uso energético global. Ambos vieron una disminución en la electricidad producida con combustibles fósiles mientras aumentaban la energía limpia. Además, el costo de las baterías, importantes para almacenar energía renovable, bajó mucho.

**Impacto** – Este cambio muestra que el mundo está entrando en una “era de la electricidad” donde más actividades se alimentan con electricidad limpia en lugar de quemar combustibles fósiles. Significa que las energías renovables ya no son una pequeña parte de la energía, sino que están tomando la delantera. Esto ayuda a reducir la contaminación y a frenar el cambio climático. Sin embargo, los combustibles fósiles todavía se usan en áreas como aviones y barcos, por lo que la transición no está completa.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos observarán si esta tendencia continúa y se extiende a otras partes del sistema energético más allá de la electricidad. Los países también trabajarán para reemplazar los combustibles fósiles en el transporte y la industria. Mientras tanto, los países en desarrollo están adoptando autos eléctricos más rápido que algunos países ricos, mostrando nuevas formas de acelerar el cambio hacia energía limpia.

**Frase clave** – En 2025, la energía renovable superó al carbón en electricidad a nivel mundial, marcando un claro avance hacia una energía más limpia y un futuro energético global cambiante.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant landscape blending solar panels and wind turbines rising gently over rolling hills, with glowing electric currents weaving through a network of stylized city silhouettes and industrial shapes, symbolizing the global shift from fossil fuels to renewable electricity, all rendered in warm, soft earth tones and simple natural colors.

articles.title: Why cities are becoming an unlikely refuge for wildflowers
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Wildflowers are associated with rolling meadows, ancient grasslands, and a pastoral world that is rapidly disappearing. The UK has lost 97 percent of its wildflower meadows over the past century, driven largely by agricultural intensification. As Nadine Mitschunas, a pollinator ecologist at the UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology, […]
The post Why cities are becoming an unlikely refuge for wildflowers first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Wildflowers are associated with rolling meadows, ancient grasslands, and a pastoral world that is rapidly disappearing. The UK has lost 97 percent of its wildflower meadows over the past century, driven largely by agricultural intensification. As Nadine Mitschunas, a pollinator ecologist at the UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology, puts it: “Arable land is heavily managed, and everything that’s not a crop is taken out.”

But the conditions that make cities hostile to most plants are exactly what wildflowers love. Stress keeps competition down, Mitschunas explains, and without competition, wildflowers can establish and spread. “They need an unstable environment, because in stable environments, only a few species survive.” A perfectly fertilised, well-watered lawn is, paradoxically, a harder place for wildflowers than a cracked pavement or a neglected roadside verge.

Cities as patchwork habitats

Part of what makes urban environments so hospitable is their variety. Pavements, walls, rooftop patches, riverbanks, and railway sidings each create their own microclimate. That fragmentation, which would be a liability in conventional ecology, becomes an asset for species that would otherwise lose out to dominant grasses or shrubs. “In urban areas they can find their niche, because there are all these specialist habitats,” says Mitschunas.

Even defining what counts as a wildflower gets complicated in this context. Cicely Marshall, a research fellow at the University of Cambridge’s Department of Plant Sciences, notes that the line between weed and wildflower is rarely objective. “A weed is any plant growing in the wrong place; one person’s weeds are another person’s wildflowers,” she says. That ambiguity is part of what makes urban ecology so interesting: a neglected strip of scrub that looks like an eyesore to one person may represent prime habitat to another.

The brownfield surprise

Nowhere is this clearer than on brownfield land, former industrial and commercial sites now largely abandoned on the edges of cities. Contaminated soil, heavy metals, alkaline ground, and low nutrients are conditions that would devastate a conventional garden. For wildflower communities, they can be ideal.

Heather Rumble, senior lecturer in healthy urban environments at the University of the West of England in Bristol, notes that the contaminants left behind by industry are not always the enemy. “Sometimes they also have pollutants that some species love, such as heavy metals,” she says. “Species have evolved to use what’s around; heavy metals are naturally occurring, we just move them and concentrate them.” Historically, miners used certain plants, sheep’s fescue and spring sandwort, to locate lead veins underground. The relationship between plants and disturbed soil is nothing new.

Low footfall, thin soil, and chemical stress together create conditions where many competing plants cannot survive, leaving space for a surprising range of specialists to move in.

What happens when wildflowers take hold

The effects reach well beyond the plants themselves. Marshall’s research at King’s College Cambridge found that a single small patch of unmown lawn, converted to a wildflower meadow in 2020, had three times as many species of plants, spiders, and insects as the surrounding grass, and attracted more bats, including more species of bats.

“The higher the population of wildflowers, the more invertebrates you have in terms of species richness and overall numbers, and this has effects up the food chain,” Marshall says. Research from Warsaw, Poland, found no meaningful difference in the diversity of species visiting urban wildflower meadows compared with natural ones, suggesting that location matters far less than the presence of the flowers.

The timing advantage of urban patches has also begun reshaping pollinator behaviour in ways researchers are still tracking. Bumblebees, which typically disappear in autumn, have now established winter colonies in cities, feeding on non-native wildflowers that bloom outside the traditional season. “In winter you also find other pollinators, like hoverflies, still active in urban areas because of wildflowers, which are a food source,” says Mitschunas.

The culture war over long grass

Not everyone welcomes the shift. Rumble identifies a social friction running through any attempt to introduce managed wildness into public spaces. “There’s a culture war over urban meadows,” she says. “Local authorities like planting them because it gives them good biodiversity credentials and they’re lower maintenance, but people complain that it looks scruffy at certain times of the year.”

That complaint, she argues, is rooted in a cultural preference for manicured green lawns that has come to define what a well-kept public space looks like. In colder months, wildflower meadows are mostly long brown grass, and for many people, that reads as neglect rather than intention.

Rumble also notes that the field has long been under-resourced: “There aren’t many urban ecologists and it’s generally an overlooked area in ecology. The research is really complicated, with people getting in the way and all the private land, but it’s getting more attention as people realise how important nature is for people.”

Mitschunas sees the attitude shift as necessary, not optional. “We need to accept a bit of wildness and untidiness. We can’t exist as humans alone; we’re part of nature and we need to let nature in.” Across the UK, local authorities are beginning to let verges go unmown, and results are accumulating. Changing the ecology turns out to be the straightforward part; the harder work is persuading people that long grass and abandonment are not the same thing.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the positive ecological impact of urban environments becoming refuges for wildflowers, which supports biodiversity and pollinators. It highlights a significant environmental benefit with broad societal implications, including increased species richness and urban ecological resilience. The story is focused, detailed, and presents a meaningful shift in urban conservation practices.
articles.personality_title: Cities provide new safe homes for wildflowers and pollinators
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Wildflowers used to grow mostly in meadows and grasslands, but in the UK, 97% of these wildflower meadows have disappeared over the last 100 years because of farming changes.

**What happened** – Researchers found that cities, with their rough pavements, walls, and old industrial lands, offer new places where wildflowers can grow. Unlike well-kept lawns, these tough city spots have less competition from other plants, so wildflowers can take root and spread.

**Impact** – This change helps many insects and animals. For example, a small city patch turned into a wildflower meadow had three times more types of plants, spiders, and insects. It even attracted more bats. Some pollinators, like bumblebees, now stay active in cities during winter because of flowers blooming outside the usual seasons.

**What's next step** – More cities are letting grass grow long and leaving places unmown to help wildflowers. The challenge is convincing people that wild, untidy areas are healthy and not just neglected. Researchers will keep studying how these urban wildflower spots affect nature and people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Urban areas, once seen as harsh for plants, are becoming important refuges for wildflowers, helping wildlife thrive in new ways.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les villes deviennent un refuge inattendu pour les fleurs sauvages et les pollinisateurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les fleurs sauvages poussaient surtout dans les prairies, mais au Royaume-Uni, 97 % de ces prairies ont disparu en 100 ans à cause des changements dans l’agriculture.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que les villes, avec leurs trottoirs rugueux, murs et anciens terrains industriels, offrent de nouveaux endroits où les fleurs sauvages peuvent pousser. Contrairement aux pelouses bien entretenues, ces endroits difficiles en ville ont moins de compétition entre plantes, ce qui permet aux fleurs sauvages de s’installer.

**Impact** – Ce changement aide de nombreux insectes et animaux. Par exemple, une petite zone urbaine transformée en prairie de fleurs sauvages avait trois fois plus d’espèces de plantes, d’araignées et d’insectes. Elle attirait même plus de chauves-souris. Certains pollinisateurs, comme les bourdons, restent actifs en ville durant l’hiver grâce aux fleurs qui fleurissent hors saison.

**Prochaine étape** – De plus en plus de villes laissent l’herbe pousser longtemps et laissent des espaces non fauchés pour aider les fleurs sauvages. Le défi est de convaincre les gens que ces zones sauvages et désordonnées sont saines et non abandonnées. Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier comment ces zones florales urbaines affectent la nature et les humains.

**Une phrase clé** – Les villes, autrefois considérées comme hostiles aux plantes, deviennent des refuges importants pour les fleurs sauvages, aidant la faune à prospérer de nouvelles façons.
articles.personality_title_es: Las ciudades se convierten en refugios inesperados para flores silvestres y polinizadores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las flores silvestres solían crecer en praderas, pero en el Reino Unido, el 97 % de esas praderas han desaparecido en 100 años debido a cambios en la agricultura.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron que las ciudades, con sus aceras agrietadas, muros y terrenos industriales antiguos, ofrecen nuevos lugares donde las flores silvestres pueden crecer. A diferencia de los céspedes bien cuidados, estos lugares difíciles en la ciudad tienen menos competencia entre plantas, lo que permite que las flores silvestres se establezcan.

**Impacto** – Este cambio ayuda a muchos insectos y animales. Por ejemplo, un pequeño espacio urbano convertido en pradera de flores silvestres tuvo tres veces más tipos de plantas, arañas e insectos. Incluso atrajo más murciélagos. Algunos polinizadores, como los abejorros, ahora permanecen activos en la ciudad durante el invierno gracias a flores que florecen fuera de temporada.

**Próximo paso** – Más ciudades están dejando crecer la hierba y áreas sin cortar para ayudar a las flores silvestres. El desafío es convencer a las personas de que las zonas salvajes y desordenadas son saludables, no abandonadas. Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo estos espacios florales urbanos afectan a la naturaleza y a las personas.

**Una frase clave** – Las ciudades, antes consideradas duras para las plantas, se están convirtiendo en refugios importantes para las flores silvestres, ayudando a que la vida salvaje prospere de nuevas maneras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant urban scene where wildflowers bloom abundantly through cracks in weathered pavements, alongside neglected brownfield patches with sparse soil, framed by silhouetted city rooftops and railway sidings, capturing the resilient beauty of nature thriving in fragmented, overlooked city habitats.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden forces are warping Earth deep beneath the surface
articles.summary: Scientists have mapped how Earth’s deepest mantle is being deformed—and the results point to long-lost tectonic plates buried thousands of kilometers underground. Using a massive global dataset of seismic waves, they found that most deformation happens in regions where these ancient slabs are thought to reside. The findings confirm long-standing theories but, for the first time, show the pattern on a global scale. It’s a major step toward understanding how the planet’s interior slowly churns over time.
articles.content:
Far beneath Earth's surface, slow-moving convection currents churn within the mantle. These currents are closely tied to the movement of tectonic plates, and they do more than just shift the plates above. They also stretch and distort the mantle material itself.

A new study published in The Seismic Record shows that much of this deformation in the deepest part of the mantle occurs in regions where scientists believe ancient tectonic slabs have sunk over millions of years.

Global Map Reveals Deformation Near the Core

Scientists had long suspected a connection between deep mantle deformation and these buried slabs, but this research provides the first global view. The team examined nearly 75% of the lowermost mantle, a layer located just above the core-mantle boundary about 2,900 kilometers (1,800 miles) below Earth's surface.

Jonathan Wolf of the University of California, Berkeley, and his colleagues built this global map using an enormous dataset. They gathered and analyzed more than 16 million seismograms from 24 data centers worldwide, creating one of the most comprehensive seismic datasets ever assembled.

Seismic Waves Expose Hidden Structure

When earthquakes occur, they generate shear waves that travel through Earth's interior. These waves move at different speeds depending on their direction and the properties of the material they pass through. This directional variation, known as seismic anisotropy, allows scientists to identify areas where the mantle has been deformed.

By studying these patterns, researchers can gain valuable insight into how the mantle flows and circulates over time.

"We know that deformation in the upper mantle is dominated by the drag of the plates that move across it. And that extremely well approximates what we know from seismic anisotropy about the deformation of the upper mantle," Wolf explained. "But we don't have any of this kind of large-scale understanding for flow in the lowermost mantle. And that's really what we want to get at."

Massive Dataset Unlocks Deep Earth Patterns

Using what Wolf describes as "the largest-ever assemblage of earthquake seismic data," the team analyzed multiple phases of seismic waves that travel down through the mantle, pass into the core, and then return to the mantle.

These waves are especially useful for mapping seismic anisotropy across distances of hundreds of kilometers, offering a clearer picture of how deformation is distributed in the deepest mantle.

The results showed anisotropy across roughly two-thirds of the regions studied. While the patterns are complex, most of the deformation appears in areas where deeply subducted slabs are thought to exist.

"This isn't that surprising in a sense, because that is predicted by geodynamic simulations," Wolf said. "But at the scale that we're looking at, it's not really been shown using those methods that we're using."

What Causes Deformation in Subducted Slabs

Scientists are still working to understand exactly why these slabs show seismic anisotropy. According to Wolf, one possibility is that the slabs retain some "fossil" anisotropy from when they were closer to the surface.

However, a more likely explanation is that intense deformation occurs as the slabs sink and interact with the core-mantle boundary. As they descend, they also push and reshape surrounding material. The extreme heat and pressure at these depths can alter the minerals within the slabs, creating a new anisotropic "fabric."

Limits of Detection and Future Research

Wolf emphasized that areas lacking a detectable anisotropic signal should not be assumed to be free of deformation. In some regions, the signal may simply be too weak for current methods to detect.

The vast dataset behind this study remains a valuable resource. Wolf described it as a "treasure trove" that researchers will continue to explore for further insights into Earth's deep interior.

"If I can dream, we will someday have enough information to really say much more about global flow directions of the lowermost mantle, knowing the seismic anisotropy across different lateral scales in the mantle, illuminating it from many directions," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding Earth's deep mantle dynamics using a massive global seismic dataset. This discovery confirms long-standing theories about tectonic plate behavior and mantle deformation on a global scale, advancing knowledge of Earth's interior processes with broad implications for geology and environmental science.
articles.personality_title: Scientists map deep Earth forces revealing hidden tectonic plates
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Deep inside the Earth, below the crust and upper mantle, lies the lowermost mantle. This region is slowly moving and changing shape because of forces linked to tectonic plates, the massive slabs of rock that make up the Earth's surface.

**What happened** – Scientists have created the first global map showing how the deepest part of the mantle is being stretched and deformed. They used over 16 million recordings of seismic waves from earthquakes, collected worldwide, to study how these waves move through the Earth’s interior. Their findings show that most of the deformation happens where ancient tectonic plates, called slabs, have sunk thousands of kilometers underground.

**Impact** – This discovery confirms long-held ideas about how the Earth’s interior moves but shows it clearly on a global scale for the first time. It helps scientists understand how the mantle flows and changes over millions of years. Knowing this is important because these deep movements affect everything from earthquakes to volcanic activity.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use this huge dataset to learn even more about the direction and speed of mantle flow. They hope to better understand how these deep forces shape the Earth’s surface and influence natural events.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have mapped deep Earth forces that reveal hidden ancient tectonic plates, improving our understanding of how the planet’s interior moves and changes over time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques cartographient les forces profondes de la Terre révélant des plaques tectoniques cachées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au plus profond de la Terre, sous la croûte et le manteau supérieur, se trouve le manteau inférieur. Cette région bouge lentement et se déforme à cause des forces liées aux plaques tectoniques, ces énormes plaques de roche qui composent la surface de la Terre.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont créé la première carte mondiale montrant comment la partie la plus profonde du manteau est étirée et déformée. Ils ont utilisé plus de 16 millions d'enregistrements d'ondes sismiques provenant de tremblements de terre, collectés partout dans le monde, pour étudier comment ces ondes traversent l'intérieur de la Terre. Leurs résultats montrent que la plupart des déformations se produisent là où d'anciennes plaques tectoniques, appelées plaques enfoncées, ont coulé à des milliers de kilomètres sous la surface.

**Impact** – Cette découverte confirme des idées anciennes sur le mouvement de l'intérieur de la Terre mais les montre clairement à l'échelle mondiale pour la première fois. Cela aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment le manteau circule et change sur des millions d'années. Cette connaissance est importante car ces mouvements profonds influencent tout, des tremblements de terre aux volcans.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'utiliser ce vaste ensemble de données pour mieux connaître la direction et la vitesse des mouvements du manteau. Ils espèrent mieux comprendre comment ces forces profondes façonnent la surface de la Terre et influencent les phénomènes naturels.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont cartographié les forces profondes de la Terre révélant des plaques tectoniques anciennes cachées, améliorant notre compréhension des mouvements et des changements à l'intérieur de la planète.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mapean fuerzas profundas de la Tierra revelando placas tectónicas ocultas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muy dentro de la Tierra, debajo de la corteza y el manto superior, está el manto inferior. Esta región se mueve y cambia lentamente debido a fuerzas relacionadas con las placas tectónicas, grandes bloques de roca que forman la superficie de la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos crearon el primer mapa global que muestra cómo la parte más profunda del manto se estira y deforma. Usaron más de 16 millones de registros de ondas sísmicas de terremotos, recolectados en todo el mundo, para estudiar cómo estas ondas viajan por el interior de la Tierra. Sus resultados muestran que la mayoría de la deformación ocurre donde antiguas placas tectónicas, llamadas losas, se han hundido a miles de kilómetros bajo la superficie.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento confirma ideas que se tenían desde hace mucho tiempo sobre cómo se mueve el interior de la Tierra, pero las muestra claramente a nivel global por primera vez. Ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo fluye y cambia el manto a lo largo de millones de años. Esto es importante porque estos movimientos profundos afectan desde terremotos hasta actividad volcánica.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar este enorme conjunto de datos para aprender más sobre la dirección y velocidad del flujo del manto. Esperan entender mejor cómo estas fuerzas profundas moldean la superficie de la Tierra e influyen en los eventos naturales.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Científicos han mapeado fuerzas profundas de la Tierra que revelan placas tectónicas antiguas ocultas, mejorando nuestro entendimiento de cómo se mueve y cambia el interior del planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of Earth's deep interior layers showing swirling, slow-moving currents within the mantle, with glowing, folded ancient tectonic slabs sinking toward the core-mantle boundary, illuminated softly to reveal subtle distortions and flowing patterns symbolizing seismic waves weaving through the dense, textured mantle rock.
